coredata.dc:title: Ratio-fluorescence detection of tert-butylhydroquinone based on non-conjugated polymer dots and gold nanoclusters
coredata.dc:creator: Peng, Bo
He, Xueyan
Wang, Chunjuan
Du, Fengxiang
Zhou, Min
Zhao, Shengguo
Fang, Yanjun
coredata.dc:description: A novel ratiometric fluorescent probe based on non-conjugated polymer dots (NCPDs) and gold nanocluster (AuNCs) was constructed to determine tert-butylhydroquinone (TBHQ). The probe exhibited dual emission peaks at 480 nm and 630 nm under 370 nm excitation. The fluorescence of AuNCs was quenched by TBHQ due to strong electrostatic interactions, whereas the emission of NCPDs increased. The ratio of fluorescence intensity at 480 nm to 630 nm (F480 / F630) was monitored as analytical signal response. The probe have been utilized for the detection of TBHQ with good linear relationship in the range of 0.2 to 60 μg/mL. The limit of detection (LOD) and the limit of quantitation (LOQ) were 0.048 μg/mL and 0.159 μg/L, respectively. Three levels of spiked-in TBHQ concentrations were obtained with recovery rates from 80 % to 102 %. The present study provided an effective ratiometric fluorescence method for selective screening of TBHQ in food samples.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Tert-butylhydroquinone
Ratiometric fluorescence
Non-conjugated polymer dots
Gold nanoclusters

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation and storage of TBHQ standard solutions
Detection of TBHQ in food samples

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors would like to thank the Research Projects of General Administration of Customs of the P. R. China (2021HK210), National Key Research and Development Program of China (2018YFC1603001), the special fund from key topics (JKBWS23J1025-3, BWS21J023) and the National Natural Science Foundation of China (22166032, 21862019) for their financial support.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Fluorescence emission spectra of NCPDs (A) and AuNCs (B) at different excitation wavelengths,(C) Fluorescence emission spectra of NCPDs and AuNCs with TBHQ concentration.(D) Stern–Volmer diagram, the inset showed a linear fit for TBHQ concentrations of 0.8 – 4.2 μg/mL.

(A) Fluorescence spectra of NCPDs, AuNCs and NCPDs/AuNCs (ratiometric fluorescent probes), (B) The Emission spectra of a ratiometric fluorescent probe (NCPDs/AuNCs) under the excitation wavelength 370 nm,(C) UV–vis spectra of AuNCs and the fluorescence spectra of NCPDs,(D) Fluorescence lifetime decay curves of NCPDs (alone) and NCPDs/AuNCs.

(A) Fluorescence spectra of TBHQ detected by ratio fluorescence sensing, the inset shows the change of fluorescence color of the reaction solution under 365 nm UV lamp. (B) The linear relationship between F480/F630 and TBHQ concentration. (C) Fluorescence spectra of AuNCs with different TBHQ concentration, the inset shows the change of fluorescence color of the reaction solution under 365 nm UV lamp. (D) The linear relationship between F0/F630 and TBHQ concentration.
Schematic diagram of ratiometric fluorescent determination of TBHQ.
Tolerance limits of different interfering substances on the TBHQ determination.

head.ce:title: Ratio-fluorescence detection of tert-butylhydroquinone based on non-conjugated polymer dots and gold nanoclusters

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: The three authors contributed equally to this work as the first author.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A novel ratiometric fluorescent probe based on non-conjugated polymer dots (NCPDs) and gold nanocluster (AuNCs) was constructed to determine tert-butylhydroquinone (TBHQ). The probe exhibited dual emission peaks at 480 nm and 630 nm under 370 nm excitation. The fluorescence of AuNCs was quenched by TBHQ due to strong electrostatic interactions, whereas the emission of NCPDs increased. The ratio of fluorescence intensity at 480 nm to 630 nm (F480 / F630) was monitored as analytical signal response. The probe have been utilized for the detection of TBHQ with good linear relationship in the range of 0.2 to 60 μg/mL. The limit of detection (LOD) and the limit of quantitation (LOQ) were 0.048 μg/mL and 0.159 μg/L, respectively. Three levels of spiked-in TBHQ concentrations were obtained with recovery rates from 80 % to 102 %. The present study provided an effective ratiometric fluorescence method for selective screening of TBHQ in food samples.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: As an oil-soluble antioxidant with patented potential, tert-butylhydroquinone (TBHQ) is an effective additive employed in a variety of products (Wang et al., 2020). TBHQ can delay the oxidative deterioration of fatty or oily foods by scavenging free radicals (Sohaib, Anjum, Sahar, Arshad, Rahman, Imran, et al., 2017). Many vegetable oils, fats and meat products might contain TBHQ in order to preserve the sensory characteristics of food such as flavor, odor and color, more significantly avoid the oxidation and deterioration of food (Fathi, Mohammadzadeh-Aghdash, Sohrabi, Dehghan, & Ezzati Nazhad Dolatabadi, 2018; Mohammadzadeh-Aghdash, Akbari, Esazadeh, & Ezzati Nazhad Dolatabadi, 2019). However, long-term exposure to TBHQ at higher doses will cause a significant risk to public health and a range of side effects, such as cytotoxic, genotoxic, carcinogenic and mutagenic effects (Khezerlou, Akhlaghi, Alizadeh, Dehghan, & Maleki, 2022). The Food and Agriculture Organization of the United Nations (FAO) and the World Health Organization (WHO) concluded that the acceptable daily intake (ADI) of TBHQ should be limited to less than 0.7 mg / kg body weight (BW) for adults and 1.3 mg / kg for infants (Negar Gharavi, Haggarty, & El-Kadi*, 2007). The national standard for food safety in China also stipulated that the maximum amount of TBHQ added as a food additive (“National Food Safety Standards: Standard for the use of food additives (GB 2760–2014),” 2014).

At present, electrochemical method (Fan, Hao, & Kan, 2018), high performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) (Li, Bi, Liu, & Sun, 2015), capillary electrophoresis (CE) (Abdollahi Aghdam, Majidi, Veladi, & Omidi, 2019), gas chromatography-mass spectrometry (GC–MS) (Ding & Zou, 2012), spectrophotometric, colorimetric method (Qiulan Li, Dezhi Yang, Sai Kumar Tammina, &. 2022) and fluorescence spectrometry(Shankar, Gowthaman, Arul, Chen, Lim, & Qin, 2021) have been extensively developed for the determination of TBHQ. These large instrumental methods can obtain reliable and accurate results in different harsh matrices. However, the requirement of expensive and complex instruments, long analysis times, and highly trained personnel limited their application in field inspection. Therefore, it was necessary to develop miniaturized analytical methods, which can lead to the advancement of more environmentally friendly, economical and more integrated systems by minimizing the necessary volume/waste of reagents (Arabi & Chen, 2022; Arabi, Ostovan, Li, Wang, Zhang, Choo, et al., 2021). Colorimetry and fluorescence spectroscopy were extensively developed for food safety monitoring and testing field due to their attractive advantages such as convenience of method construction, innovation, and simple operation (Dong, Qu, Yan, Liu, Mao, & Zheng, 2023; Liu, Zhang, Li, Du, Feng, & Ding, 2020), etc.

Recently, polymer dots (PDs) was applied in the fields of sensing, bioimaging and photodynamic / photothermal therapy (PDT/PTT) as alternatives for commercial semiconductor quantum dots and organic dyes (Mathew & Balachandran, 2023; Sajjad, Yan, Margetić, & Chen, 2021). PDs was generally divided into conjugated PDs (CPDs) and non-conjugated PDs (NCPDs) based on their bonding mode (Zhu, Song, Shao, Zhao, & Yang, 2015). Synthesis methods of CPDs included nanoprecipitation (Changfeng Wu, Craig Szymanski, & McNeill., 2006), Schiff-base condensation (Junyong Sun, Chen, Zhang, Wang, Zhang, & Gao, 2021), and microemulsion (Wu, Hansen, Hou, Yu, Zeigler, Jin, et al., 2011) etc. However, most of these methods required complex multistep preparation routes (Zhu, Wang, Li, Song, Zhao, Zhang, et al., 2014). NCPDs typically had aggregated or cross-linked polymer structures relying on its own presence of self-chromophores or unconventional chromophores, such as aliphatic tertiary amine, carbonyl, ester, and amide (CO, CN, CS) (Shoujun Zhu, Yubin Song, Xiaohuan Zhao, Jieren Shao, Junhu Zhang, Shoujun Zhu, et al., 2015; D. Wang, Imae, & Miki, 2007). Therefore, compared with CPDs, NCPDs were prepared by simpler method such as dehydration, condensation, carbonization or assembly routes (Zhu, Wang, Zhou, Zhao, Song, Maharjan, et al., 2014). The raw materials of preparation for NCPDs were more readily accessible and the synthesis processes were facile and convenient (Cai-Hong Li, Wei-Feng Wang, Nsanzamahoro Stanislas, & Yang., 2022). NCPDs were widely employed as novel fluorescent materials in many chemical and biological fields (Chunxian Zhang, Shuai Li, Zhiqiang Duan, Qin Li, Mengxuan Zhao, Yulong Chen, et al., 2022; Rafaella F. Fernandes, 2023) because they possessed distinct photostability, good water solubility, low toxicity, good biocompatibility, and environmentally friendly.

Gold nanoclusters (AuNCs) have become a popular application for fluorescence probes for their ultra-small size, easy preparation and good photostability (Peng, Su, Huang, Zuo, Yang, Li, et al., 2019). The published literature revealed that AuNCs (Shankar, Gowthaman, Arul, Chen, Lim, & Qin, 2021) and NCPDs (Han, Yan, Liu, Li, Chen, & He, 2022) had an sensitive fluorescence response to TBHQ, making the fluorescence signal going from “on” to “off” / fluorescence shut-off signal strategy. In this study, a green luminescent NCPDs and AuNCs were synthesized and first combined to construct ratiometric fluorescent probes for TBHQ quantitative measurement in biological matrix. Under acidic conditions, the Forster resonance energy transfer (FRET) effect occurs between NCPDs and AuNCs, resulting in a decrease in the fluorescence intensity of NCPDs at 480 nm and an increase intensity of AuNCs at 630 nm. As the concentration of TBHQ increased, the strong electrostatic interaction between AuNCs and TBHQ caused the fluorescence of AuNCs to be quenched, while the emission of NCPDs increased. The fluorescence color of the reaction solution changed from red to orange-red to green, suggesting the possibility of visual detection by the naked eye. The current fluorescent sensing platform provides a novel and reliable strategy for rapid analysis of TBHQ in food simples.

Tert-butylhydroquinone (TBHQ) was purchased from Aladdin. Sodium hydroxide was obtained from Tianjin Kaitong Chemical Reagent Co., ltd. Bovine serum albumin (BSA), polyvinylimide and Tetramethyl phosphorus chloride were purchased from Aladdin. Butyl hydroxyanisole (BHA), butylated hydroxytoluene (BHT), TBHQ quality control samples in edible oil (BWQ9382-2016) were purchased from Beijing Weiye Research Institute of Metrology and Technology. All regents were used as received without further purification unless otherwise specified. Ultrapure water (18.2 MΩ*cm) was provided by a Millipore water purification system.

The transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images were recorded using a Tecnai G2 F20 S-TWIN instrument (FEI, USA). The UV–visible absorption was obtained from the UV–Vis spectrophotometer (T6 new century, Beijing). Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectroscopy measurements were acquired from a Thermo-Fisher Nicolet iS10 FT-IR spectrometer. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analyses were carried out using an EscaLab 250xi spectrometer. The crystal structure of the samples was identified by using an X-ray powder diffractometer (XRD, MiniFlex600, and Rigaku, Japan) with a Cu-Kα radiation source. Fluorescence measurements were obtained on a Tecnai G2 F20 S-TWIN instrument (FEI, USA). Fluorescent photos were taken under WFH-2048 portable UV lamp (365 nm). All the photos were captured by a Samsung Galaxy S4 mobile phone (Parameter: Android 4.4.2, CPU Exynos 5410 OctaCore 1.0 GHz, GPU Imagination PowerVRSGX544, Screen 441 ppi (1920 x 1080)).

NCPDs were synthesized according to the reference method (Han, Yan, Liu, Li, Chen, & He, 2022). The specific process was as follows: Polyethyleneimine (PEI) (9.5 mL, 50 mg·mL−1) was well mixed with tetrakis(hydroxymethyl)phosphonium chloride solution（THPC）(0.5 mL, 80 %) aqueous solution and reacted at 25 °C for 1.5 h. Then the mixture was on dialysis (MW = 3500KDa) in ultra-pure water for 24 h to obtain an aqueous solution of NCPDs. The solution was freeze-dried to yield a light yellow NCPDs powder. The powder and its aqueous solution were all stored in a 4 °C refrigerator prior to use.

AuNCs were prepared using bovine serum albumin (BSA) as reducing agent and stabilizer according to the reference (Jianping Xie, 2009). HAuCl4 (5 mL, 10 mM) and BSA (5 mL, 50 mg/mL) solution were mixed and stirred at 25 °C for 2 min. The pH of the solution was adjusted by adding NaOH solution (1 mol/L) dropwise. The solution was incubate at 37 °C for 12 h. The color of the solution changed from light yellow to dark orange, and red fluorescence can be observed under 365 nm ultraviolet light, indicating that AuNCs were successfully synthesized.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation and storage of TBHQ standard solutions
ce:section.ce:para: To prepare a standard stock solution, 0.1250 g of TBHQ was individually weighted into a 50 mL amber volumetric flask and dissolved with an appropriate amount of ethanol. The stock solutions were stored in a refrigerator (4 °C) and away from light for a maximum of 1 month. TBHQ working solution was prepared by diluting an appropriate volume of stock solution with ethanol prior to use.

The ratiometric fluorescence probe was prepared as follows: 50 μL NCPDs, 150 μL AuNCs, and 1750 μL glycine-hydrochloric acid buffer solution (0.1 mol/L, pH = 2.5) were added together and diluted to 3 mL with distilled water. The solution was incubated for 2 min at 25 °C. Then TBHQ solution with different concentrations were added and continue to incubate for 10 min. The fluorescence emission spectra of the mixed solution were recorded under the excitation wavelength of 370 nm (slit widths of fluorimeter was 5 nm) by fluorescence photometer. Finally, the ratio of fluorescence intensity at 480 nm to 630 nm (F480 / F630) was calculated as the analytical signal response for TBHQ quantitative determination, where F630 and F480 represented the fluorescence intensity of AuNCs and NCPDs at 630 nm and 480 nm, respectively.

All sample were prepared according to literature (“National Food Safety Standards: Standard for the use of food additives (GB 2760-2014),” 2014). Food samples were purchased from local supermarket in Lanzhou city, China. The solid samples were finally pulverized with a grinder to form a powder before extraction.

Preparation of acetonitrile saturated n-hexane solution and n-hexane saturated acetonitrile solution: A certain amount of acetonitrile solution and n-hexane solution were added to the separating funnel, which was fully shaken and mixed. Then, the liquid was left to achieve phase stratification. The upper layer was acetonitrile-saturated n-hexane solution, and the lower layer was acetonitrile solution saturated with n-hexane.

N-hexane solution saturated with acetonitrile can dissolve the fatty acids of the sample (by n-hexane) in order to increase the liquid–liquid contact area during subsequent extraction, also reduce the solubility of antioxidants in n-hexane and increase the solubility in acetonitrile. Then extraction with acetonitrile solution saturated with n-hexane can further improve the liquid–liquid distribution efficiency of fatty acids and antioxidants in n-hexane and acetonitrile.

Liquid samples (Edible oil): First, 1 g of oil sample was accurately weighed and 5.0 mL of acetonitrile-saturated n-hexane solution were mixed in a 50 mL centrifuge tube and shaken vigorously for 1 min, then standing for 10 min. Next, 5 mL of acetonitrile solution saturated with n-hexane was added to centrifuge tube, and the mixtures were shaken to extract for approximately 2 min. Then, the acetonitrile layer was collected by centrifugation for 5 min at 3000 rpm. The acetonitrile layer in the centrifuge tube were extracted twice following the acetonitrile solution saturated with n-hexane. The collected extracts were then combined three times to be purified, do a blank test at the same time.

Solid samples: 1 g of sample powder was accurately weighed and 5.0 mL of acetonitrile-saturated n-hexane solution were mixed in a 50 mL centrifuge tube and shaken vigorously for 1 min, followed by added 5 mL saturated sodium chloride solution. Next, 5 mL of acetonitrile solution saturated with n-hexane was added to centrifuge tube, extracting for 2 min. Then, the acetonitrile layer was collected by centrifugation for 5 min at 3000 rpm. The acetonitrile layer in the centrifuge tube were extracted twice at pH = 4 following the acetonitrile solution saturated with n-hexane, the collected extracts were then combined three times to be purified, do a blank test at the same time.

C18 solid phase extraction column was filled with about 2 g of anhydrous sodium sulfate, and activated with 5 mL methanol. Next, 5 mL of acetonitrile was added to the equilibrium extraction column. The collected extracts in 2.6.1 were added to the C18 column. Then the column was eluted with 4 mL of methanol. The collected eluates were evaporated to dryness under a vacuum rotary evaporator at 40 °C under constant volume in 2 mL acetonitrile. Finally, the eluates were filtered through a 0.22 μm membrane filter prior to use.

All experiments were carried out three independent replicates. All statistical analysis was performed using SPSS and illustrations were produced by Origin 7.5. The data was expressed as mean (standard deviation (SD)). The student’s t-test was used for the statistical evaluation of the results. At a 95 % confidence level, the t -value calculated by paired Student t-test was less than t = 3.18 (95 %, 3), showing that there was no significant difference. The spiked recovery experiment were carried out and the percent recoveries calculated fell within the range of 80–102 %, showing that there was no interference from the matrix in practical samples. The tolerance limit of different substances is defined as the largest amount making a variation of less than ±5 % in the determination of TBHQ with a certain concentration.

By adjusting the excitation wavelength from 300 nm to 420 nm, the optimal excitation wavelength of the NCPDs was found to be 360 nm and the fluorescence emission peak was 480 nm (Fig. 1 A). The fluorescence emission peak of AuNCs was 630 nm under excitation wavelength 370 nm (Fig. 1B). When NCPDs and AuNCs co-existed, the fluorescence of AuNCs was quenched and the fluorescence of NCPDs was enhanced due to possible FRET effect between them which can be further verified by fluorescence lifetime experiments and zeta potential measurement. While in the presence of TBHQ, AuNCs was consumed due the electrostatic interaction with TBHQ (Shankar, Gowthaman, Arul, Chen, Lim, & Qin, 2021), and the FRET effect between NCPDs and AuNCs was inhibited. Therefore, the fluorescence intensity of AuNCs at 630 nm gradually decreased, while the fluorescence intensity of NCPDs at 480 nm gradually increased (Fig. 1C). The color of the reaction solution changed from red to orange-red to green (Fig. 1C) with the concentration of TBHQ increasing. The fluorescence intensity ratio (F480 / F630) and the concentration of TBHQ showed a good linear relationship by selecting the excitation wavelength of 370 nm. Therefore, the ratio ratiometric fluorescent probe was successfully constructed for the quantitative detection of TBHQ.

The fluorescence quenching process was generally divided into inner filter effect (IFE) (Chen, Yu, & Wang, 2018), Forster resonance energy transfer (FRET) (Zong, Wang, Mu, Wang, & Sun, 2019) and photoinduced electron transfer (PET)(Magri, 2021). As shown in Fig. 2 A, the fluorescence peaks of NCPDs at 480 nm and that of AuNCs at 630 nm were observed under excitation wavelength 370 nm. After NCPDs and AuNCs were mixed together (Fig. 2B), the fluorescence of NCPDs was quenched, while that of AuNCs was enhanced. Fig. 2C indicated the absorption bands of the AuNCs overlapped with the emission bands of the non-conjugated polymer NCPDs. Fig. 2D showed the fluorescence lifetime of NCPDs alone and in the presence of AuNCs. The fluorescence lifetime of the solution decreased with the addition of AuNCs. The fluorescence lifetime of NCPDs alone was 3.17, and after addition of AuNCs, the fluorescence lifetime of mixed solution was 0.76. Therefore, FRET may be considered as the main reason of the fluorescence variation of NCPDs and AuNCs because the IFE process generally did not alter the lifetime of NCPDs (Jian Sun, Zhao, Wang, Li, Yang, & Yang, 2018).

Zeta potential of AuNCs determination confirmed that the strong interaction occurred between AuNCs and TBHQ (Fig. S8). The zeta potential of AuNCs was −41.6 mV (Fig. S8A), resulting from the presence of free BSA carboxyl groups on the surface of AuNCs (Shankar, Gowthaman, Arul, Chen, Lim, & Qin, 2021). While the potential dropped to −1.3 mV (Fig. S8B) in the presence of TBHQ, suggesting that there was an electrostatic interaction between AuNCs and TBHQ. Besides, transmission electron microscope (TEM) images indicated the particles size of AuNCs were very small (Fig. S6A) without TBHQ, while the size increased (Fig. S6B) with the addition of TBHQ because AuNCs and TBHQ attracted each other obviously, supporting the strong interactions between AuNCs and TBHQ. Stern–Volmer mechanistic study was conducted at 20 – 45 ℃ according to the Stern–Volmer equation (Hemmateenejad, Shakerizadeh-shirazi, & Samari, 2014).
(1) F0/F = 1 + Ksv [Q]
where F0 and F represented the fluorescence intensity of AuNCs in the presence or absence of quencher, Ksv was the quencher constant, and [Q] was the concentration of quencher.

As shown in Fig. 1D, fluorescence emission data of AuNCs at different temperatures of were collected in the presence of TBHQ. F0 / F values deviated from linearity showed an upward curvature along the Y-axis. The upward curvature of Stern–Volmer curve increasing with the increase of temperature indicated the quenching process of AuNCs under the high TBHQ concentration may be controlled by two mechanisms at the same time (Seraj & Rouhani, 2017). The Stern–Volmer equation was fitted with good linearity (R2 = 0.9929 – 0.9950) in the TBHQ concentration range of 0.8 – 4.2 μg/mL (Fig. 1D). The quenching constant (Ksv) at 30, 40, and 50 °C were calculated as 0.18, 0.16, 0.15, respectively, The decreases of Ksv value with the increase of temperature suggested that the quenching mechanism involved was static quenching caused by the strong electrostatic interaction (Zhang, Wang, Jiang, & Guo, 2008). The basic mechanism of current ratiometric fluorescent detection of TBHQ was displayed in Scheme 1 .

The pH and reaction time of the fluorescence sensing system were optimized. As shown in Fig. S7A, the fluorescence intensity of AuNCs increased in the pH range 1.0 – 2.5, and kept stable from 2.5 to 4.0. The fluorescence intensity of NCPDs gradually increased with the pH from 1.0 to 2.5 and decreased from 2.5 to 4.0 (Fig. S7B). It was estimated NCPDs might aggregate to form hydrogel at low acidity (Han, Yan, Liu, Li, Chen, & He, 2022), which was not conducive to the construction of ratio fluorescent probes. Furthermore, the stability of non-conjugated polymer (NCP) in aqueous was probably affected by pH, causing the fluorescence efficiency decreased. Therefore, pH = 2.5 was selected as the optimal pH.

In consideration of the optimal excitation wavelength of NCPDs was 360 nm (Fig. S7E) and that of AuNCs was 370 nm, the fluorescence emission spectra of the ratiometric fluorescent probe without TBHQ were recorded at different excitation wavelengths from 350 to 410 nm (Fig. S7E). No significant change in the fluorescence intensity of AuNCs were found, while NCPDs presented a significant change. The tendency was consistent with the fluorescence emission spectra of NCPDs at different excitation wavelengths in Fig. 1A. Therefore, 370 nm was selected as the optimal excitation wavelength for ratiometric fluorescent probe. As shown in Fig. S7C, when the concentration of AuNCs was fixed, the fluorescence intensity of AuNCs changed as the concentration of NCPDs increased from 5 to 30 μg/mL. The fluorescence intensity remained stable when NCPDs concentration was higher than 30 μg/mL. Therefore, the concentration of NCPDs was selected to be 30 μg/mL (Fig. S7D).

The response time and stability of the ratiometric fluorescent probe were investigated. Fig. S7F showed the fluorescence intensity ratio reached equilibrium in 10 min. F480 / F630 remained basically unchanged at least approximately 50 min after reaching equilibrium, indicating the probe had good stability.

Under the optimal experimental conditions, the analytical characteristics of the ratio fluorescence method were evaluated. As shown in Fig. 3 A, with the TBHQ concentration increasing from 0.2 to 60.0 μg/mL, the fluorescence intensity of NCPDs / AuNCs probe gradually increased at 480 nm, while decreased at 630 nm. The inset illustration showed a remarkable color variation of the reaction solution under a 365 nm UV lamp.

The fluorescence intensity ratio F480 / F630 showed a good linear relationship with the TBHQ concentration in the range of 0.2 – 60.0 μg/mL (F480/F630 = 0.05c + 0.89, R2 = 0.9982) (Fig. 3B). Based on 3σ/ slope criterion (σ is the standard deviation of 10 measurements of a blank sample solution), LOD and LOQ was calculated as 0.048 μg/mL and 0.159 μg/mL, respectively. The intraday precision (n = 7) and inter-day precision (n = 7) of the method using relative standard deviation (RSD) measured with 2.08 μg/mL TBHQ were 1.5 % and 2.8 %, respectively.

The fluorescence intensity changes of using AuNCs alone as a fluorescence probe for TBHQ detection was employed and compared. Fig. 3C showed the fluorescence spectra of AuNCs with different TBHQ concentrations at the excitation wavelength of 370 nm. The corresponding solution color changes were also illustrated. The correlation linear equation of AuNCs probe was F0/F = 0.02c + 0.93 (R2 = 0.9948) in the TBHQ concentration range of 4.2–50 μg/mL (Fig. 3D). LOD and LOQ were 0.41 μg/mL and 1.37 μg/mL, respectively. The result indicated that the constructed ratiometric fluorescent probe had a much higher sensitivity and a wider linear range. It can be found the characteristics of self-correction and can efficiently eliminate blank interference because the ratio of the fluorescence intensity was employed as a response value.

Under the same optimum experimental conditions, a certain amount of anions, cations and food additives were added into the solution containing 2.0 µg/mL TBHQ to evaluate anti-interference ability of the ratiometric fluorescent probe. Common transition metals and heavy metals were chosen because they can easily quench fluorescence with extremely low content. The exploration of thiourea, EDTA, sodium citrate and F- ions were evaluated because these complexants were generally employed as efficient mask agent if cation interferences were serious. Interfering test for acetonitrile (CH3CN), n-hexane (C6H14) and methanol (CH3OH) were necessary because they were used in the sample treatment process. Sodium ascorbate, potassium sorbate, sodium benzoate, and propyl gallate (PG), butyl hydroxyanisole (BHA), hydroquinone (HQ), butylated hydroxytoluene (BHT), and octyl gallate (OG) were commonly-used food additives which might co-exist with TBHQ in matrices. The tolerance limit of different substances was defined as the largest amount making a variation of less than ±5 % in the determination of TBHQ with a concentration of 2.0 μg/mL. The results were shown in Table 1 . The result demonstrated that most of the anions and organic substances had tolerance limits higher than 3000 times. Common food additives such as sodium citrate, sodium ascorbate, potassium sorbate, sodium benzoate, PG, BHA and OG were up to 500 – 800 times. 200 times of HQ and BHT did not interfere. 100 times of Fe3+ and 50 times of Hg2+ and Cu2+ might slightly interfere. As TBHQ was often added as a food additive, the common heavy metal ions were almost not present in food with large amount. Furthermore, occurrence of Fe3+ can be masked by appropriate amount EDTA or F-. Hg2+ and Cu2+ can be masked by thiourea. In conclusion, the current ratiometric fluorescent probe had high selectivity and excellent anti-interference ability for TBHQ determination.

The comparison of the present method with the reported methods for of TBHQ determination was showed in Table S1, indicating that the ratiometric fluorescent probe displayed satisfactory results in both sensitivity and linear range. Therefore, the sensitively and precisely quantitative analysis for TBHQ can be performed with current probe.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of TBHQ in food samples
ce:section.ce:para: The proposed ratiometric fluorescent methods were applied to analyze TBHQ concentrations in different oil and food samples. As shown in Table 2 , different level of TBHQ were spiked into the samples for the recovery experiments. Each sample was accurately weighed into a 50 mL centrifuge tube, and TBHQ were added with different concentration 0.6, 1.2 and 2.4 μg/mL, respectively. The following pretreatment procedure was carried out according to section 2.6. The final sample solution was detected by ratiometric fluorescence method. The acceptable recoveries from 80.0 % to 102 % were obtained, suggesting that the TBHQ can be accurately analyzed in different practical simples.

Edible oil sample (BWQ9382-2016) Certified Reference Materials (CRM) (containing BHA, BHT and TBHQ) was used to verify the accuracy of the current method. The results in Table 3 showed that at a 95 % confidence level, the t-value calculated by paired Student t-test was 1.12, which was less than t (95 %, 3) = 3.18, proving that there was no significant difference between the present method and the high performance liquid chromatography method. The current ratiometric fluorescent method was suitable for the routine quantitative analysis of TBHQ in the practical sample with high accuracy and reliability.

A ratiometric fluorescent probe for TBHQ detection based on NCPDs and AuNCs was first established. With the addition of TBHQ, the fluorescence of AuNCs was quenched, which further led to the recovery of the fluorescence of NCPDs due to the blocking of FRET. The method had high selectivity, sensitivity, wide linear range and low detection limit towards TBHQ assay. In addition, the practicality of the proposed ratiometric fluorescent probe was verified by determining TBHQ in various oil and food samples and CRM material. The results indicated that the ratiometric fluorescent probe constructed with NCPDs / AuNCs was a reliable and efficient TBHQ detection strategy, which had great application potential in the field of food safety monitoring and inspection. However, the pH range of the probe in this study was not wide enough. The sample pretreatment was time-consuming. Besides, the strong reducibility of TBHQ may result in significant losses in sample treatment procedure and low recovery rate. The future study need to focus on expanding pH tolerance and reducing TBHQ losses in simple preparation before determination.

Bo Peng: Writing – review & editing, Conceptualization, Methodology, Supervision. Xueyan He: Writing – original draft, Methodology. Chunjuan Wang: Data curation, Formal analysis. Fengxiang Du: Data curation, Validation. Min Zhou: Software. Shengguo Zhao: Funding acquisition, Supervision. Yanjun Fang: Funding acquisition, Methodology, Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors would like to thank the Research Projects of General Administration of Customs of the P. R. China (2021HK210), National Key Research and Development Program of China (2018YFC1603001), the special fund from key topics (JKBWS23J1025-3, BWS21J023) and the National Natural Science Foundation of China (22166032, 21862019) for their financial support.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.foodchem.2024.138624.
